title: Portland City Council Work Session-Joint City/County Homelessness to Housing Continuum 2/17/26

chapters.summary: The City of Portland and Multnomah County will hold a joint work session. The subject of our meeting is our jurisdictions' roles and responsibilities in the homelessness response system.
chapters.headline: Portland and Multnomah County to hold joint work session on homelessness
chapters.gist: Joint Work Session on Homelessness

chapters.summary: Councilor Abalos: These joint sessions have proven to be really helpful for us. They've been really important for us as partners to respond to homelessness in our community. We need to keep this front and center as we are developing budgets, especially in the constrained resource environment.
chapters.headline: Council members and President Dunphy to hold joint session on homelessness
chapters.gist: County-City Homelessness Committee Meeting

chapters.summary: Councilors agreed that our first priority as a city should be on bricks and sticks, increasing housing production and homeownership. Without alignment between our two governments, we will never deliver on that promise of a region where every person has a safe place to call home.
chapters.headline: Council members will meet today to discuss homelessness and housing policy
chapters.gist: Portland City Council Holds Homelessness Work Session

chapters.summary: City Administrator Raymond Lee introduces new city administrator, Raymond Lee. Because our county colleagues are not in the Zoom meeting, I'm going to ask folks to raise their hand. We have a tight 90 minutes for discussion, and that means it's only about 5 minutes per person to talk.
chapters.headline: This is the first joint meeting with our new city administrator Raymond Lee
chapters.gist: City Administrator's First Joint Meeting

chapters.summary: The prompt questions were generally, does anyone have questions about the roles and responsibilities outlined here? What does a more unified, balanced system look like? And in the context of the recently approved revised HRS action plan and the key performance indicators, what does success look like to you?
chapters.headline: The prompt questions were generally, first, does anyone have questions about the roles and responsibilities
chapters.gist: The Overview of the HRS System

chapters.summary: The goal of this graphic is to boil down all that the county and the city do in response to homelessness, and generally who leads and/or funds each piece of that work. Please carry this forward in your one-on-one relationships with each other, into your respective budget discussions.
chapters.headline: Council President: Let's discuss the city's response to homelessness
chapters.gist: A Discussion of Homelessness

chapters.summary: Rachel Pearl is the Interim Director for Department of County Human Services. DCHS is tasked with the prevention side of the continuum. eviction prevention is our most effective tool that we have to slow the inflow into homelessness. The evictions are highest we've ever seen in the state as resources are dwindling.
chapters.headline: Rachel Pearl is the Interim Director for Multnomah County Human Services
chapters.gist: Interim Director for Multnomah County Human Services Discusses Ev

chapters.summary: In the month of January, we saw over 2,500 evictions filed in the state. Eviction prevention is also our primary strategy for family stability. Until we solve the long-term housing affordability, rent assistance is going to be a critically important tool in our belt.
chapters.headline: DCHS focuses on eviction prevention and helping families avoid eviction
chapters.gist: Multnomah County's fight against eviction

chapters.summary: Michael Bonacore: What we're talking about here is financial assistance associated with eviction legal defense, not ongoing rent assistance or vouchers. In the first two quarters of this fiscal year, 235 households maintained their housing as a result of the program. Overall it has served about 4,000 households in total.
chapters.headline: The Portland Housing Bureau administers an eviction legal defense program
chapters.gist: Portland Housing Bureau, Eviction Legal Defense Program

chapters.summary: Skyler Breckenhaupt: I'm going to go over just city outreach and then public space management. City outreach performs traditional street outreach, which includes case management, day center, shelter, and detox and treatment referrals. CHAT is under the Portland Fire Bureau. They administer light medical care, overdose response.
chapters.headline: Skyler Breckenhaupt covers city outreach and then public space management
chapters.gist: Portland Solutions: City Outreach and Public Space Management

chapters.summary: The Portland Bureau of Transportation Transportation vehicle inspection team responds to reports of lived-in vehicles. Portland Police Bureau, 24/7 emergency response, enforces city's camping regulations with outreach and IRP. The Behavioral Health Unit is also housed within the Portland Police bureau.
chapters.headline: Portland Police Bureau enforces city's camping regulations with outreach and IRP
chapters.gist: Portland's homeless services, by category

chapters.summary: The Homeless Services Department does also fund day centers. We do focus our day services where we can on priority populations. In some cases, that's assigned to that day center itself. In other cases, it's via an in-reach model.
chapters.headline: The Homeless Services Department does also fund day centers across Multnomah County
chapters.gist: Interim Director for Multnomah County's Homeless Services Department Discuss

chapters.summary: The Multnomah County funds 3 major kinds of outreach and then inreach, which is similar but different. The first is survival, so helping folks survive out on the streets. The second is service navigation, helping people get referrals to services. The third is housing placement-focused outreach.
chapters.headline: Multnomah County funds 3 major kinds of outreach and then inreach
chapters.gist: County Outreach for the Homeless

chapters.summary: Another part of our coordination and collaboration is the shelter space. We've provided over 100,000 nights of safe sleep in the last 11 months. In fiscal year 2025, the whole system served over 10,000 unduplicated people in shelter across both systems.
chapters.headline: Multnomah County Homeless Services Department funds 24/7 shelters
chapters.gist: Multnomah County's homeless shelter system

chapters.summary: Multnomah County and the Homeless Services Department fund a lot of different housing placement programs. In fiscal year 2025, just over 6,000 people left homelessness for housing across all of these programs. Right now, every month, we are placing hundreds of people into housing.
chapters.headline: Multnomah County and the Homeless Services Department fund a lot of housing placement programs
chapters.gist: Housing placement and supports for the homeless

chapters.summary: The future of this funding continues to have a very big question mark over it, as has been mentioned to you before. Most of the monies coming together and being braided and invested by the county. I hope to see this work intensifying under the renewed oversight body that Metro is putting in place right now.
chapters.headline: Council President Dunphy lays out how federal, state and local investments flow
chapters.gist: County and Local Homelessness Funding

chapters.summary: Panelists have about 5 minutes to talk. One question focuses on homelessness and behavioral health. Two new pilots related to permanent supportive housing have been announced. We're still waiting to hear where those pilots are.
chapters.headline: Panelists have 5 minutes to ask questions related to homelessness and behavioral health
chapters.gist: Discussion on Homelessness and Supportive Housing

chapters.summary: Do you know the amount of money that our— that health share cut in local behavioral health this last year? It's $100 million. Has that positively influenced Medicaid recipients' ability to access necessary behavioral healthcare? No.
chapters.headline: Are we seeing positive outcomes from cutting $100 million of behavioral health services
chapters.gist: Local Behavioral Health Services

chapters.summary: Homeless services alone cannot resolve our homeless crisis. We absolutely have to have better support support from the state in addressing unmet behavioral health needs. Multnomah County does fund employment programs that are performed by contracted providers. What that looks like will be something we're going to have to figure out together.
chapters.headline: Homeless services alone cannot resolve our homeless crisis, Commissioner says
chapters.gist: County Homeless Services: Need for employment and stability

chapters.summary: I continue to be baffled by how we don't lift and focus more on behavioral health and mental health. What happened to that commitment? I don't see it in the data. We have different models of shelter across the community. We need to find the right balance.
chapters.headline: Senator Abel Gordley was a champion for mental health and behavioral health
chapters.gist: County homelessness: Mental health and behavioral health

chapters.summary: Jillian: Why is the county choosing to cut so many shelter beds in this upcoming budget? Jillian: We did not succeed in increasing the number of people who are exiting shelter to housing. In outreach, I continue to be confused. Why don't we prioritize such things from Metro SHS funds?
chapters.headline: Multnomah County is looking to close 1,000 shelter beds in upcoming budget
chapters.gist: County to close 1,000 shelter beds in the upcoming budget

chapters.summary: There's no one-size-fits-all solution to homelessness. How are we prioritizing investments when there are proven community-supported models like Beacon Village on the chopping block? What is the projected cost savings from closing these beds?
chapters.headline: Over 1,000 shelter beds slated to be cut under proposed county budget
chapters.gist: County Homeless Programs on the Cutting Block

chapters.summary: The color scheme on the continuum, orange is the City of Portland, blue is the county. Is that to indicate just funding source, or is it to indicate who performs a function. Having two different eviction defense offices is confusing.
chapters.headline: Having two eviction defense offices is confusing, says Portland City Council member
chapters.gist: Portland County's eviction defense office unification

chapters.summary: City needs to play the role as the price maker, not the price taker in the rental market. We need to be acquiring properties as part of a social housing portfolio. And finally, I think we should be having a conversation about capitalizing a larger risk mitigation common pool resource.
chapters.headline: Councilor Green: City needs to step in and acquire social housing portfolio
chapters.gist: City-County budget discussion

chapters.summary: Jillian Heard: We have systems that duplicate services. We have fiscal cliffs we knew were coming and didn't plan for. She asks for a staff recommendation where the city and the county staff come together. Heard: I just need a directive on both sides of the river.
chapters.headline: Council President: We have systems that duplicate services that are not well coordinated
chapters.gist: County Council: We need to coordinate homelessness services

chapters.summary: Councilor Murillo: We are all facing a very difficult budget crisis. What would you want the city to focus on? Being in partnership with Multnomah County to end the homelessness of more individuals. Economic development, go for it.
chapters.headline: Councilor Murillo asks what city should focus on tackling homelessness
chapters.gist: Council President Ana Murillo on Homelessness

chapters.summary: Do we know what percentage of people who are served eviction notices subsequently become homeless? For every 1 person that ends up in eviction court, 5 self-evict. It is a challenging data point to collect accurately.
chapters.headline: Do we know what percentage of people served eviction notices become homeless
chapters.gist: How Many People Get Served With Eviction Notices Become Homeless

chapters.summary: For an adult doing PSH, the average cost is around $36,000 to $38,000 a year. Do we have a wild guess as to how many people we think should be in permanent supportive housing right now but aren't?
chapters.headline: What is the annual cost per unit of permanent supportive housing
chapters.gist: What is the annual cost per unit of permanent supportive housing?

chapters.summary: The only thing the city does is around eviction legal defense, and it is that sort of finite intervention. The county also does a lot of other rent assistance stuff. It's an area of exploration of who should do what and is there opportunity for us to streamline if we are having duplication of services.
chapters.headline: The only rent assistance the city provides is around eviction legal defense
chapters.gist: County vs. City on Rent Assistance

chapters.summary: Commissioner Berm-Edwards: There was a corrective action plan. And we had a crisis on their streets, and I think the county made both some long-term investments and also some ones that were needed right then.
chapters.headline: Commissioner Berm-Edwards: Pressure on county to make street investments
chapters.gist: County Commission: Spending on the streets was necessary

chapters.summary: I support a unified city and county strategy. On shelter and housing, respecting our roles. Both entities looking at cutting some significant shelter capacity without taking into consideration what the overall impact will be of those cuts. I think our community expects more.
chapters.headline: Jordan: I support a unified city and county strategy on shelter and housing
chapters.gist: County Commissioner on Homelessness and Housing

chapters.summary: Councilor Avalos: It's time we move from coordination to consolidation. He wants to also reinforce the idea of the additional psych beds. The city and the county need to link arms and go to the legislature and ask for those things together.
chapters.headline: Jillian Clark: I think we need to move from coordination to consolidation
chapters.gist: County Mental Health Commission: From Coordination to Consolidation

chapters.summary: The strongest driver of homelessness today is the cost of rent and the lack of available housing. The city's strongest role on the homelessness to housing continuum is on the front and the back ends. Fewer people entering homelessness in the first place is the number one success metric for the city.
chapters.headline: The strongest driver of homelessness today is structural— the cost of rent
chapters.gist: City Council on Homelessness

chapters.summary: I also want to name something that I've been hearing consistently, which is that staff are asking for clear direction. Council needs to set the vision and the policy direction, and your job, right, is to operationalize that. One of the big biggest things that we continue to lean into is just coordination.
chapters.headline: Commissioner Jones-Dixon says council needs clear direction on homelessness
chapters.gist: Councilor Koya Mullane on the Homeless Services

chapters.summary: Has there been conversation about an extended ramp-down period from 120 to 180 days? Any extension would cost money. Is there a way to think about some of our current vacant affordable housing units and see if we can give first access, first dibs to those who are losing shelter.
chapters.headline: Councilor Novick: There's talk about extending ramp-down period
chapters.gist: Councilor Novick on Homeless Housing

chapters.summary: When one-time money for housing-related stuff expires, the only pot of money we could theoretically use to make that up is the general fund. Councilor Green: These are not easy decisions, but they're critical ones that we're all going to make in the next several months.
chapters.headline: Councilor Green asks commissioners what general fund programs they would have to cut
chapters.gist: County Commissioner Jones-Dixon on Homeless Issues

chapters.summary: Commissioner Moyer: There should be a focus on reducing people who experience chronic homelessness. Councilor Avalos: In my district, there is a high concentration of people with co-occurring challenges. Mayor: There is a moral imperative to serve those people even if they cost more.
chapters.headline: Commissioner Moyer says there should be a focus on reducing chronic homelessness
chapters.gist: Commissioner Moyer on Chronic Homelessness

chapters.summary: Council President: We have coordinated with the county in the past almost every year basically on placing folks into housing. But that doesn't mean we own that program, if that makes sense. Maybe it's more colors, more charts, I don't know.
chapters.headline: Under housing placement it's all blue; everything else is county
chapters.gist: City-County Housing Coordination

chapters.summary: Mayor: My new emergency overnight shelter system has provided over 100,000 nights of safe sleep to vulnerable people. We've seen the success of our efforts reflected across the city. I will ask that we continue to fund a balance of life-saving shelter and shelter to housing options for all Portlanders.
chapters.headline: Panel: Mayor, Councilors, Commissioners discuss Portland's homelessness efforts
chapters.gist: Portland Homelessness: Performance and priorities

chapters.summary: I just want to thank everyone here at the dais for the clarity that you've provided on what you'd like to see. I also want to just say I think it's so important for us as partnering jurisdictions to provide the best support that we can to those who are experiencing homelessness. Our next session is a little more than a month away on Tuesday, April 28th.
chapters.headline: Council President: Thank you for your time today on homelessness issues
chapters.gist: County Council Discusses Homelessness
